name: Suriname

outbound_official.note: no official Suriname government outbound visa-list page located; rely on destination-side data

visa_free.nationality: CARICOM member states (Antigua and Barbuda, Bahamas, Barbados, Belize, Dominica, Grenada, Guyana, Jamaica, Montserrat, Saint Kitts and Nevis, Saint Lucia, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Trinidad and Tobago)
visa_free.notes: Under Article 46 of the Revised Treaty of Chaguaramas; CARICOM passport holders are also exempt from the entry fee. Suriname itself is a CARICOM member. Haiti is a CARICOM member but is refused entry to Suriname (see refused_entry). This entry covers the 13 other CARICOM member states only.
Vatican City passport holders are listed among countries exempt from both visa and entry fee requirements.

e_visa.notes: Entry Fee (E-Tourist Card) required; single entry, valid 90 days; must be paid online at suriname.vfsevisa.com at least 24 hours before departure; fee USD 50 or EUR 50 plus VFS service fee USD/EUR 8

refused_entry.notes: Entry and transit refused to Haitian citizens, including those who do not disembark and proceed on the same flight. Exception only for Haitian passport holders with legal residence status in Suriname (who must additionally present permission notification from the Suriname government, travel insurance, and proof of financial means). Haiti is technically a CARICOM member but Suriname applies a blanket refusal to Haitian nationals regardless of CARICOM membership.

visa_policy.notes: As of 1 July 2022, Suriname introduced a unilateral visa abolishment for tourism/family visits up to 90 days, replaced by a mandatory Entry Fee (officially called E-Tourist Card on VFS portal) of USD 50 or EUR 50 plus USD/EUR 8 VFS service fee, paid online only. CARICOM passport holders (excluding Haiti — refused entry) are exempt from both visa and entry fee. On 1 May 2023, full visa requirement was reimposed for exactly 21 nationalities (now requiring full e-visa via suriname.vfsevisa.com). Haiti is a CARICOM member but is refused entry to Suriname entirely. All travelers must also complete the Immigration and Customs Form (ICF) at icf.sr up to 7 days before arrival. The entry fee ('e-tourist card') applies to the majority of world passports not covered by the CARICOM exemption or the 21-country e-visa requirement.

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of Diplomatic, Service, Special and Official passports issued by any country are exempt from the Entry Fee requirement and may enter Suriname without paying the entry fee. No specific stay limit stated beyond general 90-day tourist rule.

Bilateral visa exemption agreement between India and Suriname for holders of Diplomatic, Official and Service Passports. Agreement signed 28 April 2017; entered into force 23 February 2019. Maximum stay: 30 days. Indian ordinary passport holders require full e-visa. Source: India Ministry of External Affairs bilateral visa waiver agreement list (mea.gov.in).

cbi.notes: No official CBI program exists. Suriname's President announced in September 2022 that a group of experts was studying the possibility of a CBI program (per news sources), but no formal program has been launched as of 2026. No official government page confirms any active CBI program.

programs.name: One Stop Unit (OSU)
programs.eligibility: Foreign nationals requiring Short-Stay Visa (MKV), Work Permit, and Residence Permit simultaneously
programs.processing_time: Approximately 3-4 weeks (versus 6+ months sequentially) assuming complete documentation
programs.notes: Launched by Suriname's Ministry of Foreign Affairs, International Business, and International Cooperation; announced October 2024, became operational mid-2025. Additional government fee of USD 1,500 beyond standard fees. All three applications (Short-Stay Visa, Work Permit, Residence Permit) reviewed concurrently. Source is Fragomen immigration alert citing the Suriname MFA; no direct official government URL confirmed.

agreements: CARICOM freedom of movement (Article 46, Revised Treaty of Chaguaramas) — applicable to 13 CARICOM members; Haiti excluded by Suriname's entry refusal
India-Suriname Visa Exemption Agreement for Diplomatic, Official and Service Passport holders (signed 28.04.2017, in force 23.02.2019)

validation_notes: CORRECTION: Haiti reclassified from 'e_visa required' to 'refused_entry'. Haiti is a CARICOM member but Suriname completely refuses entry (and transit) to Haitian nationals. flyslm.com travel requirements page and multiple aggregator sources (as of November 2025) confirm 'no admission'. The prior record incorrectly listed Haiti as e-visa eligible.

CORRECTION: Haiti removed from the CARICOM visa_free group. The prior record listed 'Haiti' inside the CARICOM free-movement entry. Since Haiti is refused entry regardless of CARICOM membership, the CARICOM entry now lists only the 13 other CARICOM member states.

CORRECTION: Colombia (COL), Morocco (MAR), and Romania (ROU) reclassified from 'e_visa (full e-visa required)' to 'e_visa (entry fee / e-tourist card)'. The gov.sr official exclusion list of countries requiring full e-visa has exactly 21 countries and does NOT include Colombia, Morocco, or Romania. These three were incorrectly placed in the full e-visa category in the prior record.

CORRECTION: China (CHN) reclassified from 'e_visa (full e-visa required)' to 'e_visa (entry fee / e-tourist card)'. Wikipedia (citing official sources) confirms China's prior visa-free agreement was rescinded 1 July 2022 and China is now in the entry-fee category, not the 21-country e-visa list.

CORRECTION: Syria (SYR) confirmed in e-visa category and given proper iso3 'SYR'. Prior record had Syria mentioned only in notes, not as a separate evisa_required entry. Now recorded in evisa_required as part of the 21-country list.
ADDITION: Vatican City (VAT) added to visa_free. Listed among countries exempt from both visa and entry fee on the official Wikipedia summary citing Suriname policy.
ADDITION: Brazil (BRA) added to the e-tourist card (entry fee) category. Multiple sources confirm Brazil's ordinary passport holders must pay the entry fee but are not in the 21-country e-visa list.

STRUCTURAL: The prior record used a single 'e_visa' array to mix two fundamentally different access types — full e-visa (requiring advance application + adjudication) and the entry fee/e-tourist card (a simple online payment). The revised record separates these into 'e_visa' (entry fee / e-tourist card, paid in advance) and 'evisa_required' (full e-visa application required). Note: The VFS portal labels the entry fee system as 'E-Tourist Card' while gov.sr simply calls it 'entry fee'. Both refer to the same USD/EUR 50 online payment mechanism.
UPDATED: The e-visa exclusion list count corrected from ~25 to the confirmed 21 countries per gov.sr and Wikipedia.
gaps: The full exhaustive list of all countries eligible for the entry fee / e-tourist card could not be scraped from suriname.vfsevisa.com (returns 403). Aggregators cite 44-52 countries. The 44-country list on flyslm.com and the broader 50+ cited elsewhere differ; the difference may be due to the addition of Brazil, Argentina, and other South American nations to the VFS portal over time.
Direct scrape of the VFS portal (suriname.vfsevisa.com) was blocked (403) across all sub-pages, so the authoritative current country count could not be confirmed from primary source.

Full list of bilateral visa exemption agreement countries (Japan, Brazil, Israel, South Korea, Philippines) could not be independently verified; consulate.gov.sr subdomains continued to return ECONNREFUSED.
No official government page for RBI/investor residence confirmed; third-party sources indicate a 5-year residency path to permanent residency but no official URL verified.
OSU fast-track program details still only confirmed via Fragomen (not a primary government URL).

The exact date Haiti was reclassified from CARICOM freedom-of-movement to 'refused entry' could not be pinpointed from official Suriname government sources; the refusal is confirmed effective at least as of November 2025.
No confirmed digital nomad visa or startup visa scheme from official sources.
No held-credential (e.g., valid Schengen/US visa granting Suriname entry) rules confirmed from any official Suriname source.
